Ezekiel stands on his feet only because the Spirit enters him and lifts him up. This is his introduction to what it means to be a prophet—not a position of honor that he earns or claims, but an assignment given to him by God after being physically empowered to receive it. The Lord immediately tells him where he is being sent: to Israel, a nation that has rebelled against God repeatedly, from their fathers' generation to the present day. These are not receptive people waiting to hear a word from the Lord. They are obstinate and stubborn, the kind of audience that most of us would consider a waste of time. Yet God is sending Ezekiel anyway, and the message is clear: speak what the Lord says, regardless of whether anyone listens. The outcome does not depend on Ezekiel's persuasiveness or eloquence. The people will "know that a prophet has been among them" simply because he speaks God's words to them. That is the measure of success in this calling.

But knowing the task ahead, God does not send Ezekiel out with false confidence. Instead, He repeats a command four times in rapid succession: do not be afraid. Do not fear the people or their words. Do not be dismayed by their presence. The imagery is visceral—briers, thorns, scorpions—the kind of hostile environment that would naturally produce fear. Yet Ezekiel is called to speak God's words into that darkness without flinching, without compromise, without counting the cost to himself. This is the calling given to anyone who would speak truth into a rebellious generation. The fear is legitimate; the command to overcome it is unconditional.

Then comes the scroll. Unrolled before Ezekiel, it contains words written on both sides—lamentation, mourning, and woe. This is not a scroll of comfort or encouragement. The message Ezekiel will carry is one of judgment, of grief, of the consequences of persistent rebellion against God. Before he even speaks a word to Israel, Ezekiel must eat this scroll, must internalize this message of sorrow. This is what will sustain him in his work: not the hope of being well-received, but the substance of God's Word itself, bitter as it may taste. The prophet does not decide whether the message should be judgment or mercy. He receives the message as it is given to him and delivers it faithfully. That is obedience. That is what God demands.
